title: The Shocking Difference Diet Makes During Pregnancy | Mini Documentary

chunks.title: Introduction to the Documentary
chunks.summary: The speaker introduces a mini documentary titled 'Nourished' in collaboration with Heart and Soil. The documentary focuses on how the quality of foods affects pregnancy, postpartum health, and preconception preparation, aiming to share valuable information with those considering pregnancy.
chunks.transcript: All right guys, I am super excited to share with you the mini documentary that I did with Heart and Soil called "Nourished." It's all about how the quality of the foods we eat affects pregnancy, postpartum, the preconception phase, and I think that a lot of the stuff you're going to see in this is going to blow your mind. Please share this with people in your life that can benefit. I want this to get the biggest reach possible. It's been available at Heart and Soil's YouTube channel for about a year, but I really wanted all of you guys to get the opportunity to see this. I don't want anyone to miss it, so enjoy this mini documentary with Heart and Soil "Nourished." Again, please share with any couples, any men, any women you know that are thinking about becoming pregnant, becoming fathers, becoming mothers who are struggling with pregnancy stuff, who are struggling with postpartum or preconception stuff. I think they're going to learn a lot from this, and I'm so proud of what we've done with this documentary. I know it's helped so many people enjoy this, guys. Let me know what you think in the comments and let me know what we should make a mini documentary about next. See you guys later.

chunks.title: Personal Experience with Diet and Fertility
chunks.summary: The speaker shares personal struggles with fertility linked to previously following a standard American diet. They discuss feelings of inadequacy and the daunting experience of unexplained fertility, emphasizing the importance of nutrition in overcoming these challenges.
chunks.transcript: To have them side by side, to feel them side by side, was just sad. We first moved to Missoula in 2014 and lived in town for about three years and then found this beautiful spot out in the country. We love the idea of growing as much of our own food as we can. Not just with the garden, but also harvesting deer or elk in the hunting season and putting that meat in our freezer. Being able to source as much of your own food locally or from your own backyard is definitely the lifestyle that we're going for. Over the last five years, I was living the standard American lifestyle, eating the standard American diet and didn't feel good, struggled to get pregnant, and didn't really know what I was doing wrong, thought I was doing all the right things. It can feel really daunting and really difficult at times, especially if you've been having fertility challenges and fertility struggles and a doctor is telling you or giving you a diagnosis of unexplained fertility, which is what I felt I was given. You think you're doing the right things and you feel like you're doing the right things, but it's not happening for you. It was a very difficult year and a half when we were trying to get pregnant and couldn't. I was very humbled in the beginning because it was very hard to experience what felt like some sort of malfunction or deficiency. And I thought, oh my gosh, there's something wrong with my body. There's something wrong and am I going to have infertility problems and all of this? I'm very grateful we went through that whole thing and I learned from it.

chunks.title: Decline in Fertility and Nutritional Impact
chunks.summary: The discussion highlights how contemporary living and diets contribute to a decline in fertility. The speaker notes the lack of nutrient-dense foods in the standard American diet and various environmental toxins that further pose challenges for reproductive health.
chunks.transcript: declining rapidly and it's pretty bad. These declines in our fertility are related to the way we're living today, clearly, both the way we eat, which is increasingly poor in nutrients and the things we're exposed to in our environments. When we look at the standard American diet, what we see is women that are consuming no nutrient-dense foods. When we don't have these nutrients, what's happening is our fertility declines every single year. There's so many different causes. We can look at the glycophates in food. We can look at the microplastics in our environment. But if we come back to supporting ourselves with food, we'll be able to move into navigating all of these environmental toxins. It's pretty scary to think about all of the endocrine disruptors we're exposed to in our environment. Xenoestrogens, phthalates in plastics, microplastics in food. We've been so far removed from what real nutrition is that we think we walk into a grocery store and the boxes that line the grocery store aisles has anything nutrient-dense in it. It's just a joke. I think there's a clear path forward and it's being aware of the environmental factors that toxins that are going to be disruptive to our fertility. The xenoestrogens, the phthalates, the plastics, the microplastics, all of these things.

chunks.title: Shifts in Dietary Understanding
chunks.summary: The speaker recounts their transition from a vegetarian diet to incorporating nutrient-dense foods, particularly organ meats, emphasizing that people can thrive with a balanced diet including animal products. They reflect on personal energy levels and overall vitality after the change.
chunks.transcript: And eating a nutrient-rich diet that avoids as many of these anti-nutrients as possible. It's one of the most gratifying things to hear that when you make these changes, people who couldn't conceive or tried everything under the sun suddenly are able to create a new life. And that's a really exciting thing. Motherhood is very rewarding. It's also the hardest job that you don't get paid for. But you get paid in kisses and cuddles and definitely a lot of attention, at least in the early years. I was vegetarian for about seven years. At the time I was so close-minded, just kind of reading headlines and not really digging into information at all. A lot of the people who I've seen follow diets that are high in grains or based on even meat alternatives or things like that. I've never seen those people to be fully thriving and myself being one of those people. I had met my husband. He did a very good job of asking questions. Maybe it's not normal to have energy so low or maybe you shouldn't need a nap every afternoon for three hours. I just kind of went full 180 and started making liver pate the next day. I don't believe that in order to be healthy and eat a nutrient-dense diet that you just have to suffer through a meal. Part of my goal in working with women, particularly in fertility and pregnancy and hormone balancing, is to kind of pair those two things together. To have healthy foods, particularly organ meats and other nutrient-dense foods. But having them in a way that is enjoyable and they can sit

chunks.title: Importance of Organ Meats in Nutrition
chunks.summary: The speaker emphasizes the nutrient density of organ meats, especially during pregnancy, and their significant role in providing essential vitamins and minerals. They advocate for traditional diets that prioritize these foods for optimal health.
chunks.transcript: nourishing themselves and at the same time want to be eating that food. Oh, a little smokey, a little smokey. See that sizzle? Cooking part is not as fun because I'm actually really nervous that it won't turn out in the way that I envision it. So there's a lot of stress involved for me. But then when it does come out, the way that I envision it, that's my favorite part. The result, seeing it like, "Whoa, I recreated this food and it came out exactly how I thought it would." I love that. There you have it. Whatever pancakes made with just beef liver, ghee, eggs, salt, topped with fresh fruit and raw honey. Depending on the age of the mother, your eggs are not viable a certain percent of the months that you menstruate. So it can range anywhere from like 25% up to like 75% for older women. For my age, I'm in my early 30s, I learned that somewhere around 40% of my eggs were just not viable based on nature. It's just you're not going to have a viable egg every month. I look back now on my fertility journey and I'm like, "Wow, we got pregnant three months in a row." I actually feel incredibly healthy and incredibly fertile and really good that my body was able to do that. People always ask me about that and I'm like, "I owe so much of our success every month to how we took care of ourselves and our diet and just the behaviors that we practice." "Bibber, bibber, who's this?" But I would eat all of these very nourishing foods, nutrient dense foods like meat, eggs, ghee, fresh fruit, liver, all of these things. And it's like I felt like they were coming into my body and like circulating and making me feel very vital and stable and nurturing me and nurturing my baby. Organ meats have always been a delicacy before we moved into the standard American diet. Organ meats are still a delicacy in many places around the world and we need to move back into wondering why that this is such a revered thing. Organ meats are some of the most nutrient dense foods on the planet. Particularly in pregnancy, many of these nutrients found in organ meats are essential for optimal brain development, for optimal bone development. So if we look at a hierarchy of nutrient presence, absolute amounts of nutrients and nutrient bioavailability, I would say meat and organs are clearly at the top of that hierarchy for absolute amounts of critical nutrients. Things like zinc, vitamin A, vitamin D, vitamin K2, choline, selenium, magnesium, calcium, iron. These nutrients are so much more bioavailable and present in greater amounts in animal foods like meat and organs than they are in vegetables. The original prenatal vitamins, so to speak, before vitamins were a thing, women would eat the organs for nutrient density. I just recently had somebody that came to me at 30 weeks of pregnancy. I just put her on a supplement of heart and liver. She texted me one week later saying that she felt the best that she's ever felt during the whole entire pregnancy. Just one week of adding this in and she

chunks.title: Comparison of Placentas from Different Diets
chunks.summary: A discussion on the comparison between placentas from women following vegetarian versus animal-based diets. The speaker describes observable differences in the placentas' quality and nutrient profiles, linking this to the nutritional intake during pregnancy.
chunks.transcript: noticed that big of a difference. Liver to me is nature's best multivitamin, but it's also nature's best prenatal vitamin. The bioavailability is everything because whatever our bodies see as artificial is just going to be what I call expensive pee. These artificial vitamins are peed out when it is not recognized within our body. Having the bioavailability of pure food and nutrient-based foods is one of the most important things that we can do for fertility and for pregnancy. Supplements synthetically created in a lab is so different than your body taking in these nutrients from real food. Your body just sees them entirely different. This is the problem with synthetic multivitamins, synthetic prenatal is they are both poorly bioavailable. A lot of the nutrients in there don't get absorbed into the human body. We just end up pooping and peeing them out. We know that the forms of many of the vitamins in these prenatal vitamins are not the forms that the body wants or uses. So what I fear with prenatal vitamins and the way that they could cause more harm than good is by giving people a false sense of security. This idea that I don't have to think about what I'm eating, I can continue eating junk food or not eating enough animal foods. If you're looking to build a diet around which nutrients you need to thrive as a human, as a mother, as a pregnant female, as a woman looking to conceive, meat in Oregon should be at the center of that diet. Taking a prenatal vitamin a lot of times gives people a false sense of security and forsakes all of the good work that women may do in terms of improving their diets. No prenatal did all organ meats, nutrient dense foods and I had a great pregnancy, baby is healthy, thriving, no problems. She's been great. Sounds perfect. Has she been moving around? Yeah. Did you feel her flip? I don't think so. I felt a lot of movement. Yeah. I feel like I'm going to have to draw her appointment on Tuesday, but I don't think I felt this specific. I feel like it's more specific to feel the head go down because it's more of like a clunk into your pelvis. Yeah. No, I don't think I felt it. I have been doing births for the last 18, almost 19 years. It incorporates into all areas of my life. People fly in from all over the world to give birth here. Hey, Lance. Come on back. How are you? Good. How are you? When people walk into my clinic, typically someone that's looking for a home birth has done a little bit more research and has a little bit more information on nutrition, but there are the people that walk in just wanting to avoid the hospital experience. And when they avoid this hospital experience, they don't realize that these recommendations for our nutrition are the most essential part of having a healthy pregnancy. Are you still doing the life blood? Yes. Okay, how often are you doing that? Every day. Perfect. Awesome. In pregnancy, when we conceive a baby, we also start growing an organ. And this organ is intended to be with this baby throughout the whole plus or minus 40 weeks. And this is what feeds the baby. This is what nourishes the baby. This is the baby's home. And we, with growing this organ, have to look at what we're putting into our bodies. Today, we had the opportunity for me to look at two placentas side by side. One being a vegetarian placenta, and the other one being a mom that was very nutrient dense with the foods that she was eating with an animal-based diet. I cannot believe the difference in the quality of the placenta, and not just the quality of the placenta, but in turn the nutrition of what the baby was growing in and what the baby received throughout the whole pregnancy. This one right here was from a mom that was primarily vegetarian throughout her pregnancy. Towards the end of her pregnancy, I was able to get her on liver pills just because her iron had dropped so significantly. And this is the placenta of a mom who was animal-based throughout her pregnancy. So she had lots of red meat, lots of pasteurized eggs, lots of raw milk. And the difference between the two, I mean, it's just obvious. There is such a huge substantial difference in the quality and the consistency of these placentas. Over here, this is called calcification. If you feel it, it's hard. There's a little bit of like a sand-like, rock-like consistency, and this is throughout the whole entire placenta.

chunks.title: Breastfeeding and Postpartum Nutrition
chunks.summary: The importance of nutrition during breastfeeding is highlighted, stressing that mothers must consume nutrient-rich diets to facilitate proper milk production. The speaker contrasts breast milk with formula and the implications for infant health.
chunks.transcript: This is not what we would want to see in pregnancy. In my almost 19 years of doing births, I've never had a side by side comparison, and it's just, it's mind-blowing to me. It's really sad that this is the start of a baby's life. This is the start of their nutrition. This is what fed them. This is what housed them. And they, you know, it's mind-blowing to see the comparison. I've had midwives and doulas tell me that when they see the placenta, which is attached to the umbilical cord of vegans, they look a lot like a smoker's placenta. And we know that the health of these critical connections, this tether between baby and mother are intimately tied to the amount of animal products in the diet. We see it over and over illustrated. If we have somebody that's eating animal-based, we had somebody that's eating grass-fed, grass-finished meat on a regular basis. If we have somebody that's eating pasteurized eggs and raw dairy, then what we're going to see is all of these vitamins, this retinol, this copper, that's all being pushed through the placenta and helping this baby sustain life. So when we look at the pregnancy piece, we also have to look at the postpartum piece. We know that when mothers are breastfeeding, that their nutrition matters just as much as pregnancy. Some mothers can't breastfeed for one reason or another. And in that case, we run into problems. There are some formulas that are better than others out there, but I think that absolutely, if someone can breastfeed, they should breastfeed. If we look at the ingredients of formula, it's hydrogenized vegetable oils, it's soy, it's artificial everything. There's actually a study that came out and it looked at SIDS. SIDS is Sudden Infant Death Syndrome. Out of every 90 babies that died of SIDS, 87 was formula-fed. Just recently, there was a formula company that had recalls because of bacteria that was causing meningitis, among other things. Breast milk is critically important. Obviously, it is the best food for babies. We want mothers to breastfeed, but in order to make enough milk, mothers need to get enough calories from a nutrient-rich diet and they need to get enough nutrients in that diet to make sure all those nutrients get passed to the baby and

chunks.title: Consequences of a Poor Diet in Feeding Infants
chunks.summary: The segment addresses the alarming links between formula feeding and Sudden Infant Death Syndrome (SIDS). The speaker shares concerns about the nutritional inadequacies in common baby formulas and advocates for breastfeeding whenever possible.
chunks.transcript: toxins in our environment, especially things like linoleic acid and seed oils and other toxins that will also be passed to the baby in the breast milk. The number one thing that I recommend is starting that nose-to-tail ancestral eating. We look at grass-fed, grass-finished meats and we look at really high-quality fats. We want to eliminate all of the poofas, the polyunsaturated fats. We want to eliminate all the vegetable and seed oils. We want to make sure that anything that they're putting in their

chunks.title: Nose-to-Tail Eating for Health
chunks.summary: The speaker promotes nose-to-tail eating practices, emphasizing the elimination of unhealthy fats and the necessity of high-quality animal products in the diet. This approach is touted as essential for mothers and their babies’ health.
chunks.transcript: based powerhouse. My daughter's name is Farah Jade Rothstein.

chunks.title: Conclusion on Dietary Choices for Healthy Motherhood
chunks.summary: Concluding thoughts reiterate the importance of returning to traditional eating practices for optimal health during pregnancy. The speaker celebrates motherhood as a natural calling and highlights the transformative journey it entails.
chunks.transcript: She's just about three months old. I had her in May here at home, had an unmedicated, beautiful home birth. Throughout my whole conception, pregnancy, labor, postpartum journey, I was on an animal-based diet. I'm doing great and my daughter's been thriving. My first one went to 41 weeks and five days. I had a midwife assisted home birth with that baby. My second child, he came spontaneously and we had a beautiful free birth right here in this room. I ate an animal-based diet through both of my pregnancies and I definitely feel like it played an important role in the outcomes that I experienced, the positive pregnancy, birth and postpartum outcomes. I have two very, very healthy children to prove it. I'm currently seven months pregnant, about 30 weeks with baby number two. My first daughter is three years old, just turned three. We've been eating an animal-based diet for about five years now and it makes us feel our best and we don't have any intentions of changing. The answer is really simple. The key to a healthy pregnancy is eating the way that humans have always eaten and not eating the way that most humans eat today. It is one of the most beautiful things to watch a woman find her power and find her strength and open up, allowing another child to come onto this planet. Being a mother is everything. I feel that it's every woman's natural calling to be a mother. There's something very primitive and natural about the process of becoming a mother. Your life before you were a mom ends very abruptly once you have a baby and then become a mother. It's for the best. It's the best thing that I have ever done
